core_meaning: よう has two main lives: (1) as a noun／na-adjective meaning 'way, manner, appearance, state' — describing how something looks or how something is done; (2) as a grammatical suffix ～ようだ／～ように meaning 'it seems that…' or 'in such a way that…'. The core idea is always <ruby>様子<rt>ようす</rt></ruby> — the shape or style something takes.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>何<rt>なに</rt></ruby>か<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>いたそうなようだったけど、<ruby>結局<rt>けっきょく</rt></ruby><ruby>黙<rt>だま</rt></ruby>ってた。
sentences.translation: She looked like she had something to say, but in the end she just stayed quiet.
sentences.notes: ～ようだ here expresses the speaker's impression from visible evidence — the <ruby>様子<rt>ようす</rt></ruby> they observed.

sentences.jp: <ruby>転<rt>ころ</rt></ruby>ばないように<ruby>気<rt>き</rt></ruby>をつけてね。
sentences.translation: Be careful not to fall, okay?
sentences.notes: ～ように used as a purpose／negative-goal construction — 'in such a way that you don't fall'; one of the most common everyday patterns for よう.

sentences.jp: <ruby>先生<rt>せんせい</rt></ruby>の<ruby>説明<rt>せつめい</rt></ruby>のしかたは、<ruby>子供<rt>こども</rt></ruby>でもわかるようになっている。
sentences.translation: The teacher's explanations are set up in a way that even children can understand.
sentences.notes: ～ようになっている describes a structure or design — 'it has come to be such that / it is arranged so that'.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>のように<ruby>話<rt>はな</rt></ruby>せるようになりたいです。
sentences.translation: I want to become able to speak like him.
sentences.notes: Two よう patterns in one sentence: ～のように ('like him') for comparison, and ～ようになる for a change of state — worth noticing the difference.

sentences.jp: <ruby>本件<rt>ほんけん</rt></ruby>については、ご<ruby>確認<rt>かくにん</rt></ruby>くださいますよう<ruby>お願<rt>ねが</rt></ruby>い<ruby>申<rt>もう</rt></ruby>し<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>げます。
sentences.translation: We humbly request that you kindly confirm the matter at hand.
sentences.notes: ～よう in formal written requests (ご～くださいますよう) is a softened imperative — more polite than ～てください; common in business email.

dont_use: Don't confuse ～ようだ (speaker's inference from evidence: 'it seems / it looks like') with ～そうだ (hearsay: 'I heard that…' or visual impression of imminent action). Also, ～のように is for comparison ('like X'), while ～のために is for purpose ('for the sake of X') — mixing these up is a classic mistake. Finally, ～ようにする ('try to do / make a habit of doing') and ～ようになる ('come to be able to do') are distinct: don't swap them.

confused_with.contrast: A common learner mistake: seeing someone about to sneeze and saying くしゃみをするようだ — this sounds like a considered inference, almost analytical. Native speakers would naturally say くしゃみをしそうだ for immediate visual impressions. ～そうだ captures 'it looks like it's about to happen right now' from direct appearance, while ～ようだ reflects a reasoned conclusion from evidence. Use ～そうだ for gut-level visual impressions; use ～ようだ when you're drawing a logical inference.

frequency: Extremely common across all registers. ～ように and ～ようだ appear constantly in daily speech, writing, and formal documents. This is a true core grammar word — you will encounter it dozens of times a day once you're at N4–N3 level.
anki_hint: よう is always about <ruby>様子<rt>ようす</rt></ruby> — the shape, look, or manner of something: ～ようだ = 'it looks like (my inference)', ～ように = 'in such a way that / like', ～ようになる = 'come to be'.
